channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A Virtual Escape for Rikers Inmates
item.description: The New York jail complex uses video games as part of its strategy to reduce violence with programming for good behavior.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Carpenter and Abdul Kircher
item.media:description: The games that are available on Rikers do not include realistic violence. Forbidden titles include those from the Grand Theft Auto and Call of Duty franchises.

item.title: ‘Michael’ Fans Danced in the Aisles, Critics Be Damned
item.description: The biopic of the pop singer crushed box-office records on its opening weekend as admirers moonwalked to a movie that critics have savaged.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Fans of Michael Jackson gathered for the world premiere of “Michael” in Berlin last week.

item.title: ‘The Lost Boys’ Review: Live, Die, Reprise
item.description: A Broadway musical adaptation of the 1987 movie gets a lot of mileage from ’80s rocker aesthetics and over-the-top spectacle — until its second half.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich

item.title: The Cult Music Documentary ‘Heavy Metal Parking Lot’ Turns Middle-Age
item.description: The film capturing the scene outside a Judas Priest show was 17 minutes long, only available on VHS and won the hearts of a generation of rock fans.
item.dc:creator: Adam M. Rosen

item.title: Courtney Washington: A New Stage but the Same Fearlessness
item.description: Courtney Washington made her name as a choreographer in street-dance competitions and on the ballroom scene. Now, she’s making a work for Parsons Dance.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert and Sabrina Santiago
item.media:description: The choreographer Courtney Washington, founder of the House of Juicy Couture.

item.title: Are Movies Really Getting Darker? Let’s Shine Some Light on the Issue.
item.description: Movie watchers are often left wondering why today’s films look murkier than films of years past. Here’s how some experts diagnose the issue.
item.dc:creator: Jessica M. Goldstein

item.title: ‘Kenrex’ Review: Plunging Into the Violent World of True Crime
item.description: The play, by Jack Holden and Ed Stambollouian at the Lucille Lortel Theater, tells the story of a brutal bully who was shot and killed in plain view.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: Jack Holden in the one-man show “Kenrex” at the Lucile Lortel Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Venice Opera Cuts Ties With the Conductor Beatrice Venezi
item.description: Beatrice Venezi’s appointment as music director drew scorn from orchestra members who said she was unqualified. She fought back in several interviews.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Arlo Brown
item.media:description: Some critics suggested that Beatrice Venezi was appointed because she was politically aligned with Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni of Italy. Supporters said she faced sexism and a politicized backlash.

item.title: Review: ‘Joe Turner’s Come and Gone,’ With Taraji P. Henson and Cedric the Entertainer
item.description: This revival starring Cedric the Entertainer and Taraji P. Henson may be uneven at times, but it still unlocks Wilson’s mysterious drama.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Cedric the Entertainer, left, and Taraji P. Henson in “Joe Turner’s Come and Gone” at the Ethel Barrymore Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Zara Larsson’s Pop Career Had Stalled. But a Big Second Act Awaited.
item.description: The Swedish singer spent a decade toiling outside the spotlight. But a PinkPantheress remix, a rainbow-streaked dolphin and an ability to roll with the punches brought her back.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Horn and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: Review: Yunchan Lim Warms Up for Graduation at Carnegie Hall
item.description: This 22-year-old superstar pianist, who is still a student, tested out his graduation recital in New York before taking it to school in Boston.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone

item.title: ‘Widow’s Bay,’ and 6 More Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: The Apple TV show starring Matthew Rhys premieres, and two true crime documentaries air.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: Matthew Rhys in “Widow’s Bay.”

item.title: Michael Jackson’s Music Was Too Big to Be Canceled
item.description: The pop superstar, who is the subject of the new movie “Michael,” faced several sexual abuse allegations. But the songs he made have endured, and even thrived.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Michael Jackson performing in 1996 in Brunei. A biopic that casts the star in a friendly light arrives this week, along with renewed accusations of sexual misconduct with children.

item.title: The Long, Challenging Road to D4vd’s Arrest
item.description: Uncooperative witnesses drove prosecutors to convene an investigative grand jury after a teenage girl’s body was discovered in the trunk of the singer’s car.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial to Celeste Rivas Hernandez. The authorities have said more than four months passed between her killing and the discovery of her body in September.

item.title: Late Night Downloads the White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The event “was supposed to be an evening of fun and merriment,” Jon Stewart said, “until, like most things in America, it was interrupted by gunfire.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Megan Thee Stallion to Leave ‘Moulin Rouge!’ on Broadway Early
item.description: The rapper will perform in “Moulin Rouge!” for the final time on Friday, though the production didn’t say why she was leaving more than two weeks early.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Herrington
item.media:credit: Theo Wargo/Getty Images For Hot Girl Productions
item.media:description: Megan Thee Stallion made her Broadway debut in “Moulin Rouge! The Musical” on March 24, and had been scheduled to appear through May 17.

item.title: Nedra Talley Ross, Last Surviving Member of the Ronettes, Dies at 80
item.description: With towering hairdos and perfect harmonies, she and her cousins Ronnie and Estelle brought a fresh edge to the girl-group sound in hits like “Be My Baby.”
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Nedra Talley (later Nedra Talley Ross) around 1970. The Ronettes, the trio in which she was joined by two of her cousins, helped define the producer Phil Spector’s revolutionary “wall of sound.”

item.title: Luis Puenzo, Director of Oscar-Winning Argentine Film, Dies at 80
item.description: “The Official Story,” set during Argentina’s brutal military dictatorship, won the country its first Academy Award for best foreign language film, in 1986.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Luis Puenzo in 1987. “It was wonderful, after so many years of censorship and people being afraid to express themselves,” he said, “to stand outside the theater in Buenos Aires and listen to the people coming out of the theater, discussing the movie, arguing among themselves.”

item.title: David Malouf, Novelist of Australia’s Divided Heritage, Dies at 92
item.description: Declared a national living treasure in 1997, he wrote poetry and short stories but was best known for his nine novels, including “The Great World.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: David Malouf in 1991. The son of immigrants, he wrote narratives that blended history, memory and fiction, and explored how Australia’s founding as a penal colony reached into a more genteel present.

item.title: The Best Books of 2026 So Far: ‘Kin,’ ‘London Falling’ and More
item.description: The nonfiction and novels we can’t stop thinking about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times Books Staff

item.title: ‘The Comeback’ Season 3, Episode 6 Recap: Everything Breaks
item.description: Valerie has been terrible at keeping the A.I. thing a secret. Almost as terrible as Billy is at being her manager.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:description: Valerie (Lisa Kudrow) is in damage control mode after spilling the A.I. beans once again.

item.title: How Will BTS Endure a Grueling World Tour? We Asked Their Ex-Trainer.
item.description: K-pop stars must develop stamina and prevent injuries while also maintaining the lithe physique their industry demands, the band’s former trainer says.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Bighit Music and Netflix, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: BTS performed a comeback concert in central Seoul in late March, their first group appearance after a four-year hiatus.

item.title: Fact-Checking ‘Michael’: What the Biopic Gets Right and Wrong About Michael Jackson
item.description: Here’s how the new biopic about the pop star handles everything from major moments like the “Beat It” video to details like a pet giraffe.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper

item.title: First-Timers Try Rooftop Roller Skating at a Children’s Museum
item.description: The Brooklyn Children’s Museum has reimagined a beloved and bygone local rink for its “Empire Skate of Mind” events. Neighborhood kids, many skating for the first time, are lacing up.
item.dc:creator: Vidhya Nagarajan and Sarah Bahr

item.title: Shayok Misha Chowdhury’s ‘Rheology’ Asks: Can Physics Soften a Mother’s Death?
item.description: In the play “Rheology,” a son and his mother grapple, in very different ways, with what her eventual death will mean.
item.dc:creator: Tim Teeman

item.title: 6 Podcasts About Classical Music
item.description: These shows demystify a genre unfairly perceived as archaic and stuffy with expert analysis, musical selections and pure fun.
item.dc:creator: Emma Dibdin
item.category: Sticky Notes: The Classical Music Podcast (Radio Program)

item.title: Poetry Month Reading Recommendations
item.description: It’s National Poetry Month! Greg Cowles, an editor at The New York Times Book Review, recommends some poetry books while writing poems with fridge magnets.
item.dc:creator: Greg Cowles, Edward Vega and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Jet Li Believes in Showing Up on Time, or Earlier
item.description: “If you tell me eight o’clock,” the film and martial arts star said, “I will be there 10 or 15 minutes before and wait.”
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Beyond Life and Death: The Way of True Freedom (Book)

item.title: $100 Million Award Made in Suit Over Unlicensed Robert Indiana Art
item.description: A New York jury found that an art publisher who made works derived from Indiana’s images had infringed on the rights of a company that had been the artist’s partner.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley
item.media:description: Robert Indiana’s most famous work, “LOVE.” There are iterations of the sculpture in several cities, including this one at John F. Kennedy Plaza in Philadelphia.

item.title: Wayne Moss, Guitarist Who Helped Broaden Country Sound, Dies at 88
item.description: A top Nashville musician, he played on Bob Dylan’s “I Want You,” Roy Orbison’s “Oh, Pretty Woman” and Dolly Parton’s “Jolene.”
item.dc:creator: Bill Friskics-Warren

item.title: Student Contest Based on Trump’s Garden of Heroes Downplays Darker History
item.description: Guidelines for the art contest, sponsored by the group Freedom 250, include material about abolitionists and civil rights leaders, but little mention of what they fought against.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: The World According to Aldwyth
item.description: In paint, bricolage and collage, she explored the history of art, ideas and the human species.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: Aldwyth, Ascetic Whose Artwork Reordered the World, Dies at 90
item.description: Holding to an austere lifestyle on a South Carolina island, she made collages and Joseph Cornell-like contraptions that told stories about art history and her place in it, and outside of it.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: 6 Shows Our Theater Critics Are Talking About
item.description: Rose Byrne in “Fallen Angels,” a couple trapped in the musical village of “Schmigadoon!” and “The Rocky Horror Show” at Studio 54: These productions are worth seeing.
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Dulany
item.media:description: Rose Byrne and Kelli O’Hara in “Fallen Angels” at the Todd Haimes Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Venice Biennale Bars Awards for Countries Facing Crimes Against Humanity Charges
item.description: The jury said it would “refrain from considering” countries whose leaders are facing charges of crimes against humanity, which would affect Israel and Russia.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: In announcing its decision to not give awards to artists from countries facing charges of crimes against humanity, the jury of the Venice Biennale said it was committed to “the defense of human rights.”

item.title: Michael Harrison, 67, Dies; Inventive Composer Who Played With Tuning
item.description: Influenced by Indian raga and nontraditional tuning systems, he created hypnotic works with a spiritual bent and created an unconventional piano.
item.dc:creator: William Robin
item.media:description: Michael Harrison performing in 2001. “Revelation,” a spellbinding and lyrical structured improvisation built from reverberant overtones, was said by a fellow composer to have been his “magnum opus.”

item.title: Review: Barbara Hannigan Is Conductor, Director and Star
item.description: The soprano Barbara Hannigan, who also directed, is multitasking at the New York Philharmonic in a disturbingly resonant take on “La Voix Humaine.”
item.dc:creator: Gabrielle Ferrari

item.title: $450 Million Worth of Newhouse Trophies Come to Christie’s
item.description: A special May evening sale will feature 16 artworks from the museum-quality collection of the Condé Nast chief S.I. Newhouse Jr.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Succession Brancusi/All rights reserved (Adagp); Photo by George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Brancusi head in the collection of S.I. Newhouse Jr., expected to sell for $100 million, is decorated with gold leaf.

item.title: ‘The Great Divide’ Review: Noah Kahan Is Caught in the In-Between
item.description: The singer-songwriter’s “Stick Season” was an unexpected smash. On its follow-up he sings about fame, complex relationships and the future with a sharper perspective.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Noah Kahan performing in February. His new album, “The Great Divide,” teems with the inner dialogues of an anxious mind.

item.title: Five Horror Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s terrors include a monkey gone mad, a boundary-pushing robot and a playground with a thirst for babysitter blood.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: This ‘Sustainable’ Island Is Venice’s Newest Art Oasis
item.description: Patrizia Sandretto Re Rebaudengo has turned a former gunpowder store into a showcase for her contemporary art collection.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Installing Pamela Rosenkranz’s “Old Tree (Pink Seas)” on the island of San Giacomo in Paludo, in Venice.

item.title: People of Note Is ‘KPop Demon Hunters’ for Gamers
item.description: People of Note has a pleasant music-themed twist on turn-based gameplay.
item.dc:creator: Harold Goldberg

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: The Restaurant World Can Be Heated. ‘Top Chef’ Is Lowering the Temperature.
item.description: More than 20 seasons into its influential run, the cooking competition series is showing a more humane touch.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams

item.title: Riz Ahmed Stars as James Bond and Hamlet
item.description: In new projects, the British actor of South Asian descent takes on two parts — James Bond and Hamlet — that have long been defining roles for white actors.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: ‘Bernstein’s Wall’ Documentary Focuses on Leonard Bernstein
item.description: “Bernstein’s Wall,” directed by Douglas Tirola, focuses on the composer and conductor’s belief that art can be an uplifting and uniting force.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: 5 Resistance Museums Recall the Bravery of ‘Ordinary People’
item.description: For many visitors to these European museums, the acts of those who opposed Nazism and Fascism have become newly relevant.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Abend

item.title: How Ashley Padilla, Breakout ‘S.N.L.’ Star, Nails Comic Timing
item.description: Ashley Padilla’s ability to extend a moment transforms what could be a routine joke into something stranger and more absurd.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman

item.title: They Were Michael Jackson’s ‘Second Family.’ Now They Say He Abused Them.
item.description: The Cascio siblings are suing Mr. Jackson’s estate after standing by him for years as he faced accusations of child molestation.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: The Cascio siblings: from left, Dominic, Aldo, Marie Nicole and Eddie.

item.title: Who’s Who in ‘Michael’: A Guide to the Characters
item.description: A new Michael Jackson biopic, with the star’s nephew Jaafar Jackson in the title role, is packed with famous musical figures. These are some of them.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Roasts Trump at a Mock White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The late night host took “a page from the Kid Rock alternative halftime show,” which aired during the Super Bowl, in offering his own “all-American” version of the Washington tradition.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Review: ‘The Rocky Horror Show’ Revival Gives Us What We Want
item.description: Sam Pinkleton’s new revival at Studio 54 gives us the big gay mayhem we want while also maintaining some order via Rachel Dratch’s droll Narrator.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich
item.media:description: Josh Rivera, center left, as Rocky, linking arms with Luke Evans as Frank-N-Furter in “The Rocky Horror Show” at Studio 54 in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Apex’ Review: Taron Egerton on the Hunt for Charlize Theron
item.description: Taron Egerton descends into full-tilt madness as a killer hunting Charlize Theron in this Netflix thriller set in southeastern Australia.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: How Shikeith Brings Pittsburgh’s Black History to the Surface
item.description: With Project Blue Space, the sculptor and image maker Shikeith brings Pittsburgh’s Black history to the surface.
item.dc:creator: Rose Courteau

item.title: Saudis Withdraw Offer of Millions to Metropolitan Opera
item.description: The arrangement would have brought up to $200 million to the Metropolitan Opera, which has suffered a series of financial setbacks.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:description: Opening night at the Metropolitan Opera in September.

item.title: Stream These Movies and TV Shows Before They Leave Netflix in May
item.description: Dozens of titles leave the streaming service for U.S. subscribers every month. Here’s a roundup of the best ones leaving soon.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey

item.title: Darrell Sheets, Auction Bidder Who Starred on ‘Storage Wars,’ Dies at 67
item.description: He appeared in 13 seasons of the long-running reality television show about storage-unit auctions. He later opened an antique shop in Arizona.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Darrell Sheets in 2013. He appeared in 163 episodes of the A&E reality series “Storage Wars.”

item.title: Ruth Slenczynska, 101, Dies; Piano Prodigy Overcame Father’s Abuse
item.description: Treated brutally, she went from celebrated child star to 15-year-old “burned-out candle.” Against the odds, she later resumed performing.
item.dc:creator: Margalit Fox
item.media:credit: James J. Kriegsmann, via Bettmann Archive/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ruth Slenczynska in 1957. Her story has been cited as a textbook example of the toxic consequences that can result when a controlling parent and a gifted child collide.

item.title: After Cesar Chavez’s Fall, What to Do With the Art That Honored Him?
item.description: Artists who created public depictions of the civil rights icon Cesar Chavez have had to revisit their works after accusations emerged of Mr. Chavez’s sexual abuse of girls in the movement.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango and Ariana Drehsler
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times

item.title: Michael Tilson Thomas, Celebrated American Conductor, Dies at 81
item.description: A galvanizing force in classical music as a conductor, composer, pianist and evangelist, he spent 25 years as music director of the San Francisco Symphony.
item.dc:creator: Anthony Tommasini
item.media:credit: Hiroyuki Ito for The New York Times
item.media:description: Experimentation and ambitious projects marked Mr. Thomas’s years at the San Francisco Symphony. He conducted the orchestra in a Stravinsky program at Carnegie Hall in 2018.

item.title: Meet the Turner Prize 2026 Nominees
item.description: This year’s shortlist for the major British art award includes an artist who stages spoken-word performances and another who makes art using oil company merch bought off eBay.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall

item.title: Anne Hathaway, Queen of Effort, Is Finally Ready to Vibe
item.description: Known best for tightly wound characters in generational hit films, the actress is a tortured pop star in “Mother Mary” and returns to playful form in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Thea Traff
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
